headline: Shohei Ohtani Goes First Overall to North Correa, and Abbotsford-Colby Twins snagging Blake Snell at Pick 172 (ADP 98) Was the Steal of the Draft
subheadline: From Ohtani going first overall to Blake Snell falling 74 picks, the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft had more drama than a season finale — here's who won, who lost, and who needs to seriously reconsider their life choices.
body:
# The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review
### *By Bill Simmons, The Ringer*

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is on the phone making seventeen trades simultaneously while everyone else in the room is sweating through their shirts? That's every fantasy draft room, except instead of Oakland's future, you're gambling with your pride, your trash-talk credibility, and — in leagues like this one — probably some actual money. The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft went down on March 22nd, and fourteen managers sat down with their cheat sheets, their ADP spreadsheets, their half-finished beers, and their varying levels of delusion, all convinced they were about to build a champion. Some of them were right. Most of them were not.

Here's what I can tell you upfront: this draft had everything. It had a generational talent going first overall with zero controversy. It had a defending champion double-dipping on steals like they were at a Vegas buffet. It had a team — and I won't name them yet, but their mascot involves a medical drama — that reached so hard in Round 8 that I physically winced. And somewhere in the back half, **Blake Snell** fell seventy-four picks past his ADP like a guy who showed up to his own surprise party three hours late. Seventy-four picks. That's not a steal, that's a heist. That's *Ocean's Eleven* if Danny Ocean also had a 3.12 ERA.

The big storyline heading in was simple: who was good enough, smart enough, and caffeinated enough to actually challenge the back-to-back contention window? **Sugar Land Skeeters** won the whole thing in 2025. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** won in 2024. **BaseballPro77** won in 2023. This league has a rotating championship culture, which means everyone thinks they're one good draft away. Let's find out who actually is.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**North Correa** held the first overall pick and did exactly what anyone with functioning baseball knowledge would do: they took **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** — the singular unicorn of modern baseball, slotted as a Util in the Los Angeles Dodgers lineup — and never looked back. There was no drama here, no hot takes needed. Ohtani as a batter in 2025 posted numbers that make other fantasy players look like they're playing a different sport. This was the Kevin Durant-to-Golden-State pick: correct, obvious, and deeply unsatisfying to debate. The rest of Round 1 followed predictable tier logic: **Athletic Park Dragons** grabbed **Aaron Judge** (still the most terrifying right-handed bat in baseball) at two, **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** got **Juan Soto** at three (coming off a massive Mets contract year), and **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — the 2024 champs — landed **José Ramírez** at four, which is honestly a gift given what JRam does across five categories.

The pivot point of Round 1 came at pick seven, where **Hiroshima Radiation** went pitching-first and grabbed **Tarik Skubal**, who was arguably the best starter in baseball in 2025 with a sub-2.80 ERA and a FIP that made advanced metrics people visibly emotional. Right behind them, **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** took **Paul Skenes** at eight, making picks seven and eight the most pitcher-heavy back-to-back selections since the league started going early on arms. Meanwhile, **Buttville Skidmarks** got **Elly De La Cruz** at nine — still just scratching the surface of his potential — and **General Hospital** grabbed **Kyle Tucker** at ten, which given Tucker's 2025 campaign (33 HR, elite OBP, Dodger lineup protection) looks like tremendous value at that slot. **Sugar Land Skeeters**, the defending champs, got **Garrett Crochet** at eleven, the best pure strikeout arm available after the top two starters were gone.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** | *Draft Slot: 1* | **Grade: B+**
You get **Ohtani** at one — congratulations, you've correctly identified water as wet. The question is what happens next, and honestly? It's a mixed bag. **Mookie Betts** in Round 2 is a reach at 11 picks above ADP (Betts's injury history makes that sting), and **Chris Sale** in Round 3 at 10 picks early is the kind of bet that either wins you a championship or leaves you refreshing the IL wire in May. **Jack Flaherty** in Round 10 is a 30-pick reach that I can't fully justify. The saving grace is **Jeff Hoffman** in Round 13 — a 21-pick steal for a reliever with legitimate closing value — and **Oneil Cruz** in Round 7 as a power-speed flier. North Correa has been a bottom-feeder (14th in 2024, 12th in 2025) and this draft feels like a swing for the fences after years of treading water. Ohtani covers a lot of sins, but the middle rounds lack the depth to complement him. *They built a beautiful penthouse on a shaky foundation.*

**Athletic Park Dragons** | *Draft Slot: 2* | **Grade: C+**
**Aaron Judge** at two is correct. **Cristopher Sánchez** in Round 2 is smart, low-key value. **James Wood** in Round 3 is a fascinating boom-or-bust add on one of Washington's better young bats. The problem? This team finished 14th in 2025 after finishing 6th in 2024, which means the wheels fell off hard, and this draft doesn't scream "we figured it out." Three reaches in the back half — **Willson Contreras**, **Dansby Swanson**, and **Gleyber Torres** — all arrive above their ADP, and none of them are players who move the needle enough to justify the urgency. Swanson posting a .234 average last year while reaching at pick 142 is... a choice. *Vibes of a team that peaked at pick two and hope Judge carries them to September.*

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | *Draft Slot: 3* | **Grade: B-**
**Juan Soto** to open, **Bryan Woo** in Round 2 (smart, quietly excellent), **Trea Turner** in Round 3 (speed, pop, the full package). The foundation is legitimately good. **Mason Miller** in Round 4 as a closer adds NSVH upside. The reach on **Brandon Woodruff** in Round 9 (18 picks early) is a gamble on health that has burned fantasy managers before — this dude is made of glass and great intentions. But **Taylor Ward** in Round 11 as a 15-pick steal? Love it. And **Matthew Boyd** as a Round 13 stash? Questionable, but the upside exists. Wisconsin finished 10th in 2025 in their first tracked season and this roster feels like a step up — solid floor, real ceiling questions. *A good-not-great draft from a team trying to announce itself.*

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | *Draft Slot: 4* | **Grade: A-**
The 2024 champions are BACK and they drafted like it. **José Ramírez** at four, **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2, **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 3 — that's a three-round knockout punch that would make Hagler-Hearns people nod respectfully. Then they snagged **Blake Snell** in Round 13 at 74 picks below his ADP. *Seventy-four.* I cannot stress this enough. If Snell is healthy and pitching like his peak self, that's a Round 2 asset sitting in your Round 13. The only dings: **George Springer** in Round 4 is a 36-pick reach (Springer's injury history is a legitimate concern at this investment level), and **Brandon Nimmo** in Round 9 is somehow 38 picks early. But listen — when you're stealing Snell in the 13th round, you've earned some forgiveness. *Defending champs who drafted like they remember what winning feels like.*

**BaseballPro77** | *Draft Slot: 5* | **Grade: B+**
Two championships in four years and this team drafts with a confidence that comes from knowing what they're doing. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five, **Ketel Marte** in Round 2, **Logan Gilbert** and **George Kirby** back-to-back in Rounds 3-4 — that's pitching infrastructure that would make any GM smile. **Luis Castillo** in Round 7 is 29 picks early (the only real eyebrow-raiser), but the rest of the board is disciplined and deep. **Mike Trout** in Round 13 is a pure lottery ticket — if healthy, massive value; if not, you spent a late pick on hope. **Giancarlo Stanton** in Round 14 is the same bet. For a contending team, this draft has the profile of someone who respects the process. *A methodical, experienced draft from a team that knows the middle rounds win championships.*

**Albuquerque Bombers** | *Draft Slot: 6* | **Grade: C**
Look, **Ronald Acuña Jr.** coming back from injury at pick six is a calculated bet on upside, and **Francisco Lindor** in Round 2 is excellent. But then we get into some murky water. **Christian Yelich** in Round 6 is 24 picks early on a player who's been a shell of his 2018-2019 self. **Zack Wheeler** in Round 8 is 32 picks early. **Sandy Alcantara** in Round 9 is 38 picks early on a pitcher coming off Tommy John. **Carlos Rodón** in Round 12 is 24 picks early. That's four reaches of 24+ picks across the back half of the draft. The Bombers finished 3rd in 2025, so they're not rebuilding — they're swinging. But reaching this aggressively on injury-prone veterans feels less like a strategy and more like wishful thinking. **Jorge Polanco** as a steal in Round 15 is a nice consolation prize. *A team that fell in love with upside stories and paid for them with draft capital.*

**Hiroshima Radiation** | *Draft Slot: 7* | **Grade: B**
Going pitching-first at seven with **Tarik Skubal** is a defensible and frankly excellent choice — Skubal was the best SP in baseball last year and has mid-20s durability. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 2 adds power and OBP, **Bryce Harper** in Round 3 is still elite when healthy, and the sneaky pick here is **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)** in Round 8. Yes, a different manager took Ohtani the batter first overall, and Hiroshima turned around and grabbed Ohtani the pitcher in Round 8. That's either genius or chaos, and honestly it might be both. **Roman Anthony** in Round 4 is a high-upside Boston prospect. **Nathan Eovaldi** in Round 9 is a 16-pick reach on a guy whose career has been a medical thriller, but the upside exists. After finishing 5th in 2025, this team has the look of a legitimate contender. *Skubal + Ohtani (Pitcher) is a starting rotation flex that belongs in a museum.*

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | *Draft Slot: 8* | **Grade: A**
Runner-up in 2025, top-five finishes in three straight years, and they just had arguably the best draft in the room. **Paul Skenes** at eight is a foundational ace. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 2 creates an elite pitching duo. Then they grabbed **Pete Alonso** in Round 3 at pick 36 — 14 picks below his ADP of 22. Alonso with Baltimore, coming off a bounce-back campaign, and the Wonderdogs got him at a Round 3 discount. **Josh Hader** fell 12 picks to Round 10, giving them a closer of rare distinction. **William Contreras** behind the plate in Round 4 is a legitimate fantasy catcher. This roster has pitching depth, power, saves upside, and a manager who clearly does homework. The only mild concern is positional versatility in the middle infield, but I'm nitpicking. *The Wonderdogs might be the best-constructed roster in the league and they've got the track record to prove it's not an accident.*

**Buttville Skidmarks** | *Draft Slot: 9* | **Grade: B-**
**Elly De La Cruz** at nine is great — elite speed, improving power, the kind of player who wins SB categories by himself. **Junior Caminero** in Round 2 is a fascinating power prospect. **Wyatt Langford** in Round 3 and **Rafael Devers** in Round 4 give this team real offensive depth. The concerns: **MacKenzie Gore** in Round 10 is 17 picks early on an arm that has never fully delivered on its promise, and **Bryan Reynolds** in Round 12 is 21 picks early on a player who profiles as a 2-category contributor in H2H. **Spencer Strider** in Round 11 is a pure lottery ticket after his 2024 TJ surgery. The Skidmarks have hovered around 8th-11th for three years — good enough to not embarrass themselves, not good enough to win. This draft is more of the same: talented but not quite championship-calibrated. *Middle-of-the-pack energy with a couple of bold bets that could either break the ceiling or confirm the floor.*

**General Hospital** | *Draft Slot: 10* | **Grade: C-**
Back-to-back 13th place finishes. Let that sink in. And then let this sink in: **Luis Robert Jr.** in Round 8 at pick 103, which is 46 picks above his ADP of 149. Forty-six picks. Robert missed significant time in 2025 and his career has been a highlight reel interrupted by an injury documentary. Taking him 46 picks early is the single most aggressive reach in this entire draft. **Kyle Tucker** at ten is excellent. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** in Round 2 is a massive talent. **Manny Machado** in Round 3 and **Max Fried** in Round 4 round out a top-four that's genuinely strong. But then the wheels come off in the back half — Robert, **Cam Schlittler**, **Bubba Chandler** — the depth feels thin and speculative. For a team that's finished 13th twice in a row, you need this draft to be a home run. It's more of a solid double with a baserunning mistake. *Strong top of the roster, baffling Round 8, and the historical record of a team still searching for its identity.*

**Sugar Land Skeeters** | *Draft Slot: 11* | **Grade: A-**
The defending champions drafted with the swagger of someone who knows exactly what they're doing, because they DO know exactly what they're doing. **Garrett Crochet** at eleven — the best strikeout arm available at that slot — followed by **Fernando Tatis Jr.** in Round 2, **Hunter Brown** in Round 3, and then the steal of early picks: **Matt Olson** in Round 4 at pick 46, falling 12 picks below his ADP of 34. Olson posted 40+ HR last year. Getting him in Round 4 borders on criminal. Then **Edwin Díaz** in Round 6, 20 picks below ADP. **Ryan Helsley** in Round 11, 14 picks below ADP. The Skeeters have a documented history of finding closers late — they did it with Díaz and Helsley before, and they're doing it again. **Max Muncy** in Round 12 is a 30-pick reach, but you've earned that indulgence when you're this disciplined everywhere else. *The defending champs drafted like defending champs, and the rest of the league should be nervous.*

**Riverside Roughnecks** | *Draft Slot: 12* | **Grade: C+**
**Jackson Chourio** at twelve is excellent value — he's 22 years old, hit .280 with 24 HR in 2025, and has four-category upside. **Nick Kurtz** in Round 2 is a compelling first base prospect. But **Cole Ragans** in Round 3 at 14 picks above ADP raises an eyebrow, and **Trevor Story** in Round 7 at 25 picks above ADP is the kind of injury gamble that keeps fantasy managers up at night. The saving grace is the late-round bullpen strategy: **Carlos Estévez** in Round 13 at a jaw-dropping 51 picks below ADP, and **Kenley Jansen** in Round 14 at 22 picks below. The Roughnecks have been a 9th-11th place team for three straight years — solid, never spectacular — and this draft has that same energy. **Shane McClanahan** in Round 15 is a nice stash if healthy. *A team that builds competently but hasn't yet found the move that separates them from the middle of the pack.*

**Canberra Wombats** | *Draft Slot: 13* | **Grade: B+**
The Wombats have the most eclectic steal history in the league — **deGrom**, **Altuve**, **Jo Adell**, **Aaron Nola** — and they did it again in 2026. **Gunnar Henderson** at thirteen is elite value (30 HR, .780+ OPS, multi-position eligibility). **Cal Raleigh** in Round 2 is the best catcher in fantasy baseball not named Adley Rutschman. Then the steals start: **Jacob deGrom** in Round 5 at 13 picks below ADP (if he's healthy, that's a top-20 SP for a Round 5 price), **Jose Altuve** in Round 9 at 27 picks below ADP, **Jo Adell** in Round 12 at 26 picks below, and **Aaron Nola** in Round 15 at 17 picks below. Five steals is not luck — that's a philosophy. The Wombats finished 4th in 2025 after winning in 2023, and this draft has the look of a team ready to reclaim the throne. *Nobody finds late-round value like the Wombats, and one of these years they're going to run the table because of it.*

**Benchwarmers** | *Draft Slot: 14* | **Grade: B**
Last pick, but the snake giveth: **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen and **Corbin Carroll** at fifteen means the Benchwarmers opened with two young OF cornerstones back-to-back. That's a massive haul for the 14th slot. **Zach Neto** in Round 3 and **Brent Rooker** in Round 4 add pop and positional flexibility. **Cody Bellinger** in Round 5 is a 13-pick steal — if the Yankees version of Bellinger shows up, that's massive value. **Emilio Pagán** in Round 14 at 23 picks below ADP continues a pattern of the Benchwarmers finding late-round bullpen value (see: previous **Pagán** mentions in their steal history). After finishing 9th in 2025, this feels like a genuine step forward. *A smart draft from a team that maximized their snake-draft position and found value in the turns.*

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE THREE BEST PICKS:**

🏆 **Blake Snell, SP | Abbotsford-Colby Twins | Round 13, Pick 172 | ADP: 98 | Fell 74 picks**
This is the pick of the entire draft. Full stop. Snell is a former Cy Young winner being drafted in the 13th round because managers got skittish. The Twins — defending champions, by the way — patiently waited and pounced. If he's healthy, this is a Round 2 starter at a Round 13 price. The Dodgers don't give rotation spots to people who can't pitch.

🥈 **Carlos Estévez, RP | Riverside Roughnecks | Round 13, Pick 180 | ADP: 129 | Fell 51 picks**
A proven closer falling 51 picks in a league that scores NSVH is borderline inexcusable for every team that passed on him. Estévez is a legit fantasy closer asset and the Roughnecks grabbed him in Round 13. That's how you win the back half of a draft.

🥉 **Matt Olson, 1B | Sugar Land Skeeters | Round 4, Pick 46 | ADP: 34 | Fell 12 picks**
40+ HR. Elite RBI accumulator. First baseman who does nothing but rake. Fell to Round 4. The defending champs took him and smiled. This is the kind of foundational mid-round pick that separates championship rosters from almost-championship rosters.

**THE THREE WORST REACHES:**

💀 **Luis Robert Jr., OF | General Hospital | Round 8, Pick 103 | ADP: 149 | Taken 46 picks early**
The worst reach in the draft, and it isn't close. Robert's injury history is extensively documented and this is a team that finished 13th for two consecutive seasons reaching 46 picks early. This is the fantasy equivalent of going all-in on pocket twos.

💀 **Brandon Nimmo, OF | Abbotsford-Colby Twins | Round 9, Pick 116 | ADP: 154 | Taken 38 picks early**
The Twins' only real blemish in an otherwise strong draft. Nimmo is a fine player — good OBP, occasional power — but taking him 38 picks above ADP in Round 9 when the Twins are already spending draft capital on George Springer's injury risk feels redundant.

💀 **George Springer, OF | Abbotsford-Colby Twins | Round 4, Pick 53 | ADP: 89 | Taken 36 picks early**
Listen, the Twins had a great draft overall, which is why they appear twice here — the highs were high enough to still earn an A-minus. But Springer in Round 4 at 36 picks above ADP, given his recurring injury history, is the kind of pick that can unravel a strong roster construction in April.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

**1. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs win the championship.**
Runner-up last year, top-five three straight years, deepest pitching staff in the league (Skenes, Yamamoto, Valdez, Imanaga), and two of the best late-round steals (Alonso, Hader). This is the year the Wonderdogs close the deal.

**2. North Correa finishes outside the top six despite having Ohtani.**
The middle rounds weren't deep enough. Three reaches in Rounds 2, 3, and 10, combined with a historical trend of bottom-feeding, suggests Ohtani can only carry this team so far. He'll win them categories every week, but the rotation and depth will cost them.

**3. Blake Snell becomes the most-talked-about player in the league by June.**
Abbotsford-Colby Twins got him for a song in Round 13. If Snell is healthy and pitching like vintage Snell, the league will spend three months complaining that the defending champs cheated the ADP system. They didn't cheat — everyone else just wasn't paying attention.

**4. General Hospital's Luis Robert Jr. misses 60+ games**, and that Round 8 reach haunts their entire season. Strong top of the roster (Tucker, Vlad Jr., Machado, Fried) gets undermined by a single panic pick. History repeats for the back-to-back 13th place finishers.

**5. Canberra Wombats finish in the top three**, powered entirely by their steal-heavy philosophy. deGrom (if healthy), Altuve, and Aaron Nola in Round 15 will be talked about the way their deGrom steal was talked about previously. The Wombats don't win drafts on the first pick — they win them on the last ten.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Give me the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**.

Here's the case: They've finished 4th, 5th, and 2nd in three consecutive seasons. They know what almost-winning feels like, which means they know exactly what they were missing. This draft answered every remaining question. **Paul Skenes** and **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** at the top of a rotation is a legitimate 1-2 punch that can win ERA, WHIP, K, QS, and IP on its own. **Pete Alonso** — stolen at 14 picks below his ADP in Round 3 — gives them a 40-HR first baseman. **Josh Hader** as a closer, also stolen in Round 10, handles the saves-and-holds category. **William Contreras** is a top-five fantasy catcher. **Michael Harris II** is a sleeper. The middle infield is thin, sure, but **Geraldo Perdomo** and **Ozzie Albies** provide enough floor.

Most importantly, this team has never made an irrational reach. Every pick has a purpose. Every late-round add has upside. In a 14-team H2H league where the middle rounds decide the championship, the Wonderdogs draft like they've read every page of every analytics textbook and then actually understood it.

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** are the defending champs and they drafted brilliantly — Crochet, Tatis, Olson, Díaz, Helsley, all legitimate contributors — so if the Wonderdogs stumble, the Skeeters are right there to collect another trophy.

But my money is on Wonder Wharf. They've been knocking on the door for three years, and this is the draft of a team that finally decided to kick it in.

*The Wonderdogs didn't just draft a roster — they drafted a statement.*

---
*MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball. Drink responsibly. Draft better.*
